actions.thought: I am dealt 2h 5d, which is an extremely weak, unsuited hand with no playability. In a three-handed game, even on the button, there is no reason to invest chips into a pot with such low-ranking cards. My strategy is to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand to enter the pot, as conserving my stack is crucial in the early stages of a 150-hand tournament.

In a blind-on-blind situation after the button folds, Ks 4s is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) allows me to take the initiative, put pressure on the Big Blind, and capitalize on the hand's suited potential if called.

Opponent raises from the Small Blind, and we hold a mid-pocket pair (77) in the Big Blind. Calling is the standard and most profitable play here to see a flop in position. We can effectively set-mine given the deep 100 BB stacks, and our pair still has solid showdown value against a wide SB opening range if the board runs out cleanly.

I have a King-high flush draw on a board of Qs 8d 5s. As the pre-flop aggressor, I should continuation bet to maintain initiative and capitalize on my equity. A bet of 300 into a 600 pot (50%) is a standard sizing that applies pressure while allowing me to semi-bluff with a strong draw.

On a Qs 8d 5s board, our 77 is third pair. The small blind's continuation bet range includes Qx, 8x, higher pocket pairs, and numerous flush/straight draws that have excellent equity against our hand. With two overcards to our pair and many bad turn cards (spades, Broadway cards) that will make it impossible to continue against further aggression, our hand is too weak to call even in position. The best decision is to fold and preserve our stack since we missed our set.

92o is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although I am on the button, which is the best position, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify a raise or a call. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With A5 offsuit in the small blind and the button folding, I have a decent hand to attempt a blind steal or play a heads-up pot with the initiative. Raising to 300 (3 big blinds) puts pressure on the big blind. If called, I have an ace and some playability post-flop.

My hand (6s, Tc) is too weak to defend against a raise from the small blind. Being early in the tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack for higher equity situations rather than playing a marginal hand out of position.

Playing 3-handed, I am on the button with 98o. While I have position, 98 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability post-flop if I get called. There is no need to force a steal this early in the tournament with a marginal holding. I will fold and wait for better starting hands.

Holding pocket sevens in the small blind against the big blind after the button has folded, I have a significant range advantage. Raising to 300 allows me to capitalize on this advantage, take the initiative, and potentially fold out weaker overcards or build a pot with a hand that plays well heads-up.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8d 2s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Flashlight raised from the Small Blind to 300. Although I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand has very poor equity and playability post-flop. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding 3c 8c on the button is an extremely weak starting hand with very little playability. Even in a blind-versus-button situation, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such poor cards. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have 97 suited in the small blind after the button folded. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds immediately, or play a pot with initiative if called.

Flash raises 3x from the small blind. I am in the big blind with K2 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand with poor post-flop playability and significant reverse implied odds if an King flops. Even in a blind-versus-blind scenario, K2o is an easy fold against a 3x open.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. While the button allows for a wider opening range to steal the blinds, T5o is generally too weak to open profitably against two active opponents. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I have 75o in the Small Blind. Flash on the Button folded. This is a very weak hand (unsuited two-gapper) and playing it out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. While a steal attempt could sometimes work, 75o has very little equity if called. Folding is the most standard and solid play here to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am dealt T3o on the button. This is a very weak hand that lacks high card strength, connectivity, and suitedness. Even though I have position, playing this hand would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

With a very weak holding of 8d 4h in the small blind, continuing to play against the big blind out of position is negative expected value. Preserving my stack early in the tournament by folding the small blind is the most disciplined and professional decision.

I have been dealt pocket Aces, which is the strongest starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Being in the button position, I want to build a pot and capitalize on my hand strength. A standard open raise to 300 chips is appropriate here to define my range and put pressure on the opponents in the blinds.

KQo is a very strong hand in 3-handed play, especially against a button open which is typically wide. I want to build a pot and take the initiative out of position. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard size to put pressure on the opener and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate the hand.

Q8s is far too weak to call a 3-bet out of position, especially facing a strong reraise from the small blind and with the original raiser still to act behind me. Folding is the only sensible option here.

Holding pocket Aces (As, Ac), I have the best possible starting hand. After opening to 300, the Small Blind (Flash) 3-bet to 1000. A 4-bet is the standard and most profitable move here to maximize value, protect my hand, and build a large pot. A 4-bet to 2500 puts significant pressure on the opponent's range and commits them to the hand while building toward an eventual stack-off.

KQo is a strong hand in 3-handed play, but facing a 4-bet from the button after my 3-bet is a high-pressure situation. Flashlight's 4-bet to 2500 represents a significant portion of their stack (around 1/3) and indicates extreme strength, typically JJ+ or AK. Against that range, KQo has roughly 25-30% equity. I am also out of position for the rest of the hand. Although the pot odds are close, the risk of being dominated or facing a stack-committing bet on the flop makes this a disciplined fold to preserve my stack.

5c 7h is a weak, offsuit hand that does not play well post-flop. While I am on the button in a three-handed game, this hand is at the bottom of the range and doesn't merit a steal attempt, especially considering Flashlight's recent aggressive play. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

With pocket deuces in the small blind facing only the big blind, limping (completing the blind) is a standard and effective strategy. It keeps the pot small out of position while allowing us to see a cheap flop with the primary goal of hitting a set. Raising might invite an aggressive 3-bet from the big blind, forcing us to fold our equity or play a bloated pot out of position with a marginal holding.

I am in the big blind with Q3o. The button folded and the small blind completed the bet. Since it is a limped pot, checking is the standard play to see a free flop with my hand. Raising would be unnecessary given the weak strength of my holdings and the potential for a wide range from the small blind.

We missed our set on a very wet and high-card heavy flop (Qh Kh 3c). Out of position with bottom pair (pocket deuces) and no redraws, we have almost no equity against any piece of this board. The best play is to check and likely fold to any bet, as our opponent's wide big blind checking range connects well with these high cards.

I have bottom two pair (Queens and Treys) on a dry, rainbow board. Since the opponent checked to me, this is a prime opportunity to extract value from hands like a single King, a weaker Queen, or any speculative hands that paired up. Checking would grant a free card that could potentially improve the opponent's equity, so I will make a value bet.

We completely missed the flop and face a bet from the Big Blind on a very wet, high-card heavy board (Qh Kh 3c). Our pocket deuces have virtually no equity, no draws, and we are out of position. The easiest and most optimal play here is to fold and preserve our stack for a better spot.

AQs is a premium hand, especially in a 3-handed game on the Button. Opening with a standard 3x raise to 300 is ideal to build the pot, take the betting lead, and thin the field while holding a very strong hand.

I am in the small blind with 3s4s, which is a very weak hand to play out of position against an open raise from the button. Even though it is suited, playing 3-high against a standard opening range is high-variance and generally unprofitable. Folding here is the most disciplined and correct strategic choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9d 5d. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. With the Small Blind folding, it is 200 more for me to call into a pot of 450. I am getting roughly 2.25:1 odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 650). 95 suited is a reasonable hand to defend the big blind with against a button opening range, as it has some post-flop playability and flush potential. Although I will be out of position, the price is right to see a flop.

I have middle pair (5s) on a 6h-5s-3h board. This board texture is generally better for my defending range than the Button's opening range. However, out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their action. I have a clear continue if Pro bets, but for now, checking is the best tactical move.

The board of 6h 5s 3h heavily favors the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many small pocket pairs, suited connectors, and one-gappers. With AdQd, I missed the flop completely and have no immediate draws. Betting here as a continuation bet will likely run into many strong hands or draws that will not fold. Checking behind for pot control allows me to realize my equity by seeing a free turn card, where I might hit an Ace or Queen.

I have middle pair (5s) on a board that now has some straight possibilities (any 4 makes a straight). Pro checked back the flop, which often indicates a capped range of overcards or a marginal made hand. By checking the turn, I continue to control the pot size and can evaluate Pro's action. If Pro checks behind again, I likely have the best hand going into the river. If Pro bets, I have a reasonable bluff catcher given the flop check-back.

The turn card brings a 2s, making the board extremely connected (2-3-5-6) and putting a 4-straight out there. Flash has checked again, indicating weakness or a tentative draw, but this board hits a Big Blind calling range fairly well (many small pairs and straight draws). I have Ace high with a gutshot to a wheel straight (a 4 makes a straight). Taking a free river card is the most prudent play, as a bet might only get called by better hands or draws with high equity, and we still have showdown value with Ace-high against missed draws.

The board has paired (2d), and Pro has checked back both the flop and the turn, which suggests a weak range or a hand with some showdown value like high cards or a small pair. My pair of fives (forming two pair with the deuces on board) has significant showdown value. I do not believe betting will get called by many worse hands, and checking allows me to capture the pot if it goes to showdown or potentially catch a bluff if Pro decides to bet his missed high cards.

The river pairs the deuce, and the board is very connected but Flash has checked all three streets. My Ace-Queen high has decent showdown value against missed heart and spade draws, or random King-highs. A bet here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and better hands (like small pairs or a 4) probably won't fold since the pot is small and Flash's range is weak. Checking behind ensures we see a showdown without risking more chips on a bluff.

Holding 3s8c in the button position is a weak hand that lacks sufficient equity to open the pot in a three-handed game. It is best to fold and wait for a more playable starting hand to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with J9o. The Button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). J9o is a solid hand to open-raise with from the SB to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately. A standard raise to 300 (3x the BB) is appropriate here to take control of the hand.
I have 85 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. This hand is too weak and disconnected to defend or 3-bet, making it a clear fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo), which is a premium hand in 3-handed play. I have the best position at the table. Standard strategy is to open-raise to apply pressure on the blinds and build the pot with a strong range. I will raise to 300, consistent with my previous opening sizes in this tournament.
Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a button raise to 3 big blinds, folding is the only profitable play, especially being out of position in the small blind.

I am in the big blind with pocket kings, which is a premium hand. Given the button has opened, I want to 3-bet to take control of the hand, build a larger pot, and discourage flatting with speculative hands. A raise to 1100 is a standard sizing to maximize value.

AQo is a premium hand in a 3-handed game, especially on the button. After Flashlight's 3-bet from the Big Blind, I have a strong enough hand to continue. Calling is generally better than 4-betting here because it allows me to use my positional advantage on the flop and keeps Flashlight's bluffs or dominated hands (like AJ, AT, or KQ) in the pot. If I 4-bet, I might force out the hands I beat and get shoved on by hands that have me crushed. I will call the 1100 to see a flop in position.

The flop is Ace-high, which hits the calling range of my opponent significantly. Since I do not have an Ace, my pocket Kings are essentially a bluff catcher at this point. Betting here is likely to get called only by hands that have me beat (any Ace) or better, and checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn. If my opponent bets, I will have to decide whether to continue based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent path.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (AQ) on an Ace-high board in a 3-bet pot. Flashlight's check after being the pre-flop aggressor is interesting; it could be a trap with a set or AK, or it could be pot control with a hand like AJ or KK. Checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot and see a turn card for free. It also protects my range and can induce bluffs from Flashlight's air or missed draws on the turn and river. Since I am in position, I can comfortably call a lead on many turn cards.

With an Ace on the board and having checked the flop, my pocket Kings are largely a bluff catcher. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands (any Ace) or strong draws, while checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the river. My opponent's check on the flop indicates they might not have an Ace, but I still cannot confidently bet for value.

I have top pair with an excellent kicker (AQ) on an Ace-high board. Flashlight has checked twice after 3-betting pre-flop, which suggests either a marginal made hand (like AJ, AT, or KK/QQ) or a drawing hand that missed. By betting now, I can extract value from those marginal hands and charge any flush draws. Checking back again would likely lose too much value, especially since I need to protect my hand against the clubs on the board. A bet of 1100 into the 2250 pot is a standard sizing to get called by worse Aces or draws while keeping the pot manageable if I am somehow behind to AK or a set.

The opponent's check on the flop is a key piece of information; it significantly reduces the likelihood that they hold an Ace, as an Ace would typically value-bet on an A-T-8 board. Given their turn bet of 1100 into a 2250 pot, it looks more like a probe or a medium-strength value bet (like JT or 8x). Since I am holding pocket Kings, I have showdown value against their potential bluffs or inferior pairs. Calling the 1100 is the most logical path to see the river, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to re-evaluate on the final street.

The board is extremely dangerous with an Ace, Queen, and three clubs present. My pocket Kings are significantly weakened by these cards. I have no value betting range here as I am likely behind any Ace, Queen, or flush. I will check to see the opponent's action, and plan to fold to any significant bet given the board texture.

On the river, the Queen of Clubs (Qc) improves my hand to two pair (Aces and Queens). However, this is a very dangerous card as it completes both a possible flush (three clubs are now on the board) and a possible straight (KJ). Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and then check-called my turn bet, which is consistent with holding a strong Ace or a high-card flush draw. By checking back here, I ensure I reach showdown with a very strong hand that beats all of Flashlight's single-pair hands (like AJ or AK without a flush) while avoiding a potentially expensive check-raise from a flush or a straight. Given the board texture and the action, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity.
I am dealt 72 offsuit on the button, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The correct play is to fold immediately and save my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (Queen-Four offsuit) after the button folded. In a small blind versus big blind scenario, this hand does not have enough equity to justify a raise or a completion against an aggressive big blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

J6 offsuit is a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability. Even in a 3-handed game on the button, this hand does not have enough equity to justify calling a raise or even limping. It is better to fold and conserve my stack for a more advantageous hand.

With the button folding, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation against Pro. T6s is a reasonable hand to open for a raise from the Small Blind to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop. I've successfully stolen the blind from Pro previously (Hand #11) and T6s has decent playability if called.

I have a premium hand (AKo) in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open. This is a clear 3-bet for value to build the pot and punish a likely wide SB opening range. Sizing to 900 (3x the open) is standard to generate fold equity or extract value from weaker aces and broadway hands.

I opened with T6s from the Small Blind to apply pressure, but Pro responded with a 3-bet to 900. While T6s is a suited hand with some playability, calling a 3-bet out of position against the Big Blind with a marginal hand is generally a losing play. I would be forced to play a large pot with a hand that is likely dominated or behind Pro's 3-betting range. To protect my stack and wait for a more advantageous situation, I will fold and concede the initial 300-chip raise.

I am on the button with KJo, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is healthy at 11,800 chips. Standard play here is to raise to 3x the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and build a pot with a superior range. Both Pro and Flashlight have shown some willingness to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands, so a standard open to 300 is appropriate.

A8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. Calling from the small blind against a button open invites the big blind to squeeze, and even if we see a flop, we are often dominated by better aces or playing a guessing game. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but we have better candidates for that. Folding is the most solid and standard decision here.

My hand is 2d 6d, which is extremely weak and lacks playability, especially out of position against a button raise. The pot odds of 200 to win 450 are not sufficient to justify a call with such a weak holding. A disciplined fold allows me to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the Button in 3-handed play and first to act. My hand, Ten-Four suited, is far too weak and disconnected to open-raise or call, even from the dealer position. The standard and optimal play is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Three offsuit. This is a very weak hand in an heads-up blind-versus-blind situation. While I have already posted 50, playing this hand out of position against the big blind is strategically unsound as it lacks value and playability. Folding is the disciplined, low-variance decision here to preserve my stack.

I am holding 4c-6s on the button. This is a very weak, uncoordinated hand that does not offer strong equity, even in a three-handed game. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to preserve my stack for better hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 3d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. The Button has folded, making this a blind-versus-blind situation. While I could consider a raise to steal the Big Blind, 2-3 offsuit has very little playability if called. Given my healthy stack, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with such a poor hand. I will fold.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight suited, which is a strong hand for opening in a 3-handed game. I have a healthy stack and want to exert pressure on the blinds. A standard open raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing and is appropriate here.

I am in the Small Blind facing a 3x open raise from the Button. My hand, Queen-Four offsuit, is far too weak and disconnected to play, especially out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I hold 8s-2d, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call for a 450 pot), defending the big blind with such a dominated hand out of position is a losing strategy. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more suitable opportunity.
T2o is a very weak starting hand, especially pre-flop. Folding is the only optimal decision here.

With Ace-Six offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I have a clear opportunity to steal the blinds and take initiative heads-up. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind and build a pot with a superior hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 6h. The button folded and the Small Blind (Flashlight) raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call the additional 200, Q6 offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Flashlight has shown some aggression in previous hands, and I would rather wait for a better spot than defend with a marginal hand like this.

My starting hand of 7d 5h is weak and unsuited, offering little potential for improvement or value. As I am in the button position, it is best to conserve my stack by folding, as this hand is not strong enough to warrant an open-raise or a call in this spot against my opponents.

With the button folding, I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind. 87 offsuit is a standard hand to attempt a steal with in this position to put pressure on the BB. I have had some success stealing blinds from Pro in previous hands, so I will raise to 300.

Q2o is a weak, disconnected, off-suit hand. Facing a raise from the small blind, there is no value in calling or re-raising with such a marginal holding, especially considering the lack of playability post-flop. Folding is the correct move here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While I have been aggressive in previous hands, Ten-Four is significantly below the threshold for a profitable opening range, even in a three-handed game. I have a healthy chip lead and there is no need to play such a weak hand out of position post-flop or risk a three-bet from either of the blinds.

54o is a very weak starting hand, even in a blind-vs-blind situation. With Flashlight in the big blind, raising as a steal or limping with such weak, offsuit, low cards has a negative expectation. The best decision here is to simply fold and save our chips for a better opportunity.

On the button, 4h 9h is a very weak hand. Even though it is suited, it lacks high card strength and connectivity, making it unprofitable to open or call with against the blinds. Folding is the correct standard play.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h-2h, which is an extremely weak hand. Given that I am out of position against the Big Blind (Flash) and the hand value is minimal, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack rather than attempting to play a dominated hand out of position.

I am dealt pocket eights on the button in a 3-handed game. Pocket eights are a strong hand in this short-handed format. I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and discourage the blinds from seeing a cheap flop. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to accomplish this while maintaining control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 2d. The button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. Jack-Deuce offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Even against a standard button opening range, this hand is well below the threshold for a profitable call or a 3-bet bluff. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
K4o is a very weak hand, particularly out of position against a 3x Button raise. The best course of action is to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am on the button with 7h 2d, which is widely considered the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I have been aggressive from the button in previous hands to pick up blinds, there is no reason to risk chips with a hand that has such poor equity and playability post-flop, especially since my opponents (Pro and Flashlight) have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With Flash folding on the button, the action is on me in the Small Blind. Queen-Nine offsuit is a decent starting hand for a Blind versus Blind situation. Rather than limping and playing out of position post-flop, I'll put in a standard raise to 300 to try and steal the Big Blind immediately.

Pro has raised from the small blind, which is a wide opening range. With suited cards Jh 7h in the big blind, I have sufficient pot odds (2:1) to defend. Raising is unnecessary and potentially risky with this hand, so a call is the standard defensive play to see a flop.

The board of 3d-4d-2c favors the Big Blind caller's range significantly, connecting with small pocket pairs, suited connectors, and A-x hands they might defend with pre-flop. I missed completely, holding only Q-high with a backdoor flush draw. Betting here is likely to be called or raised, so I will check to evaluate my opponent's action.

The flop is extremely low and coordinated, which significantly favors the small blind's opening range. My hand (Jack-high) has no equity, no pairs, and no draws on this board. Since the opponent checked to me, checking behind is the most standard and disciplined play to control the pot size and avoid putting chips in with a hand that has very little chance of winning at showdown.

The turn card puts four to a straight (2-3-4-5) on the board. Any Ace or 6 makes a straight, and both of these cards are very common in the Big Blind's pre-flop defending range. My Queen-high has no real showdown value and essentially zero equity. Attempting a bluff into a board texture that hits my opponent's range this perfectly is highly risky and unprofitable. Checking is the only sensible option; if Flashlight bets, I will easily fold.

The board now shows a 2-3-4-5 sequence, which makes any 6 or Ace a straight. My hand (Jack-high) has no showdown value and no pair. Pro checked to me on the turn after checking the flop, suggesting they likely have a marginal hand or are playing passively. Betting into this board with Jack-high is high-risk and offers little chance of forcing a fold from a range that might include a small pair or an Ace. Checking behind is the most disciplined action to keep the pot small and see the river for free, minimizing potential losses while I have a weak hand.

The river brings the 7c, completing a runner-runner flush and putting a four-liner to a straight on the board. My hand is still Queen-high, which has no showdown value. Attempting to bluff into this board when my opponent could easily hold an Ace, a 6, or a club would be reckless. Checking and surrendering the pot if faced with a bet is the optimal play here.

The board texture is highly connected, creating a straight possibility with any six (2-3-4-5-6). My hand, holding a pair of 7s, is extremely marginal. Given that my opponent has checked the river, it is highly likely they are either pot-controlling a marginal hand or checking to induce a bet. Betting here with a weak pair is unlikely to yield value, as most hands that would call a bet are better than mine. Checking is the most disciplined and safe play to reach the showdown with the least risk of losing more chips.

With Q3 suited on the Button 3-handed, we are at the very bottom of our potential opening range. While we could sometimes attempt a steal, folding is the safer and more standard play, especially against aggressive opponents in the blinds.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand, 2s 4s. Given the position and hand strength, it is mathematically optimal to fold and minimize losses rather than contest the pot out of position against the big blind.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand (Jack-Trey offsuit). This hand has no showdown value and poor playability. Folding is the most profitable decision to conserve my stack for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Q9o. The action has folded to me. In a blind-vs-blind situation, Q9o is a strong enough hand to warrant a raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially win the pot immediately. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

74o is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand with virtually no playability or equity, even when defending the big blind against a small blind steal. The optimal play is to simply fold and preserve chips for a better starting hand.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. While I have the positional advantage, this hand is quite weak and doesn't play well against two opponents who have been active in previous hands. Raising would be a speculative bluff, and calling just to see a flop with such a weak hand is generally negative expected value (EV) in a 3-handed game. I'll choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

With QJo in the Small Blind and the action folded to me, I have a very strong hand for a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to pressure the Big Blind, take control of the hand, and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. The pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win a pot of 400), making this a mathematically sound spot to defend with a hand containing an overcard. I will call the raise and look to play post-flop.

With QJo, I completely missed this 8-7-3 flop. This board texture often connects well with the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many middling cards, pocket pairs, and straight draws. Out of position with no draw and only overcards, a continuation bet would often get called by better hands. Checking is the safest play, allowing me to potentially see a free turn or easily fold if my opponent shows aggression.

The flop is dry and does not connect well with my King-high holding. Since the opponent checked, I have the option to take a free card or check back to maintain pot control. Betting here as a bluff has poor risk-to-reward because the pre-flop raiser's range typically contains many pairs or overcards that will call a bet. Checking is the most prudent action to preserve my stack.

The 2s on the turn doesn't improve my hand, and the board still favors the Big Blind's defending range. Since my opponent checked behind on the flop, they might have a marginal made hand, a draw, or just high cards. I will check again with my Queen-high, looking to see a free river or easily fold to any aggression. Bluffing out of position on this texture is unlikely to be profitable.

I am facing a check on the turn with King-high and a gutshot straight draw.
